topic.what: compositional security definition for higher-order programs with where-declassification
topic.how: use binary logical relations indexed by labeled types and Kripke worlds, with formalization of relevant declassification steps that capture when to stop enforcing indistinguishability
topic.why_matters: existing higher-order where-declassification definitions are noncompositional, preventing modular verification of libraries; this work enables compositional reasoning and reuse of security proofs

characteristics.what_it_measures: permissiveness of security definitions for first-order programs
characteristics.evidence: Theorem 5.1: our definition implies Flow-Lock security for first-order programs
characteristics.context: proved for first-order state restriction; compositionality does not weaken security

characteristics.what_itasures: correctness of typing rules with respect to semantic model
characteristics.evidence: every typing rule is semantically admissible when replacing syntactic typing with semantic typing
characteristics.context: establishes mixing type-based and semantic proofs is sound; witnesses compositionality

characteristics.what_it_measures: whether secure partial programs compose into secure whole programs
characteristics.evidence: logical relation treats functions as indistinguishable when extensionally indistinguishable; applies to all language constructs
characteristics.context: enables modular verification: verify each library function once, reuse proof everywhere

characteristics.what_it_measures: ability to accept intuitively secure programs rejected by conservative type systems
characteristics.evidence: example function that conditionally writes to low variable controlled by high input is accepted as secure
characteristics.context: without relying exclusively on type systems for compositional verification

characteristics.what_it_measures: number of semantic definitions required to cover language features
characteristics.evidence: logical relation consists of value and expression relations indexed by types and Kripke worlds
characteristics.context: compact model compared to store-based approaches

characteristics.vs_baseline: FG base language plus 6 declassification constructs
characteristics.evidence: Figure 1 syntax: unit, N, sums, pairs, refs, functions, open/close/when constructs
characteristics.context: extends FG with scoped open, close, and when constructs for declassification

good_for: higher-order programs requiring compositional security proofs
requires: first-order state restriction for comparison to Flow-Lock security

novelty: first compositional security definition for higher-order where declassification
formalization of relevant declassification steps in logical relations
embedding Flow-Lock knowledge-based conditions into expression relation
lifting store-based security to value-based compositional setting
demonstration that compositionality does not weaken security (is stronger)
open_problems: extending to when declassification (currently only where)
proving termination-sensitive logical relation is equivalence relation
generalizing relevant declassification to other intensional declassification styles
handling higher-order state in knowledge-based security definitions
